caveats: a: No defensible compensation-to-receipts denominator was provided.
a: Specialty-crop automation evidence spans outdoor orchards and nurseries as well as covered food crops, while crop labor intensity varies widely.
rho: Yield, quality, and energy improvements are not all labor substitution.
rho: Glass greenhouses, low-tech high tunnels, hydroponics, sprouts, and vertical farms have different control and data maturity.
e: The frozen dataset explicitly provides no defensible LMM firm count, so this conditional share cannot establish target volume.
e: The 2,994 operations threshold was only $10,000 in horticultural sales and operations are not firms.
s5: All-farm producer age is not owner age or transaction intent.
s5: Horticulture ownership data include floriculture and nursery operations beyond 111419.
q: Vegetable-market evidence includes open-field production and processing vegetables.
q: Tomatoes, cucumbers, lettuce, herbs, berries, sprouts, and specialty crops have different pricing power and spoilage.
d5: The physical growth comparison ends in 2019 and sales data are nominal and older.
d5: Operations, production weight, and constant-quality customer demand are different quantities.
o: Imports or consolidation can displace domestic operators without eliminating operator-required growing.
o: Some retail or foodservice buyers may vertically integrate supply, shifting rather than eliminating the operator.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat wholesale and direct sales to grocers, foodservice distributors, processors, institutions, produce markets, and consumers, priced by crop, grade, package, season, contract, delivery, freshness, local attributes, and prevailing fresh-produce markets.
lens.excluded_activities: Mushroom production, nursery and floriculture, open-field crops, passive real estate, pure distributors, captive growing units, research facilities, non-control financing vehicles, and operations without transferable control.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. operating businesses repeatedly growing and selling vegetables, fruits, melons, sprouts, hydroponic crops, and other food crops except mushrooms under greenhouses, vertical farms, high tunnels, or other protective cover to external customers.

a.bridge: USDA NIFA documents specialty-crop automation for sensing, irrigation, disease scanning, and harvest assistance rather than wage-weighted task exposure in 111419. USDA describes controlled-environment systems that regulate temperature, wind, light, and precipitation. I translate these capabilities into remaining exposure across climate control, irrigation, crop scouting, grading, planning, and administration. Because greenhouse work remains labor-intensive and many systems already automate climate and fertigation, the proxy can overstate novel AI while understating manual crop handling.
caveats: No defensible compensation-to-receipts denominator was provided.
Specialty-crop automation evidence spans outdoor orchards and nurseries as well as covered food crops, while crop labor intensity varies widely.
a.change_evidence: A task-and-compensation study of U.S. greenhouse, hydroponic, vertical, and high-tunnel food operations by crop and facility type, separating installed controls from remaining work, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can optimize climate, lighting, irrigation, nutrients, energy, crop timing, disease detection, yield forecasts, inventory, labor scheduling, sales, and records. Seeding, transplanting, pruning, trellising, harvesting, packing, sanitation, repairs, food safety, and biological judgment remain physical or human-led.

d5.bridge: USDA ERS reports protected-crop operations rose from 1,476 in 2009 to 2,994 in 2019 and production increased 56% to 7.86 million hundredweight, while NASS reports 2019 protected food-crop sales of $703 million, down 12% from 2014. I translate the long-run physical expansion and more recent sales weakness into a five-year constant-quality demand ratio, widening for imports, energy, capital, and crop-mix uncertainty. Historical expansion may overstate future growth after recent investment and failures.
caveats: The physical growth comparison ends in 2019 and sales data are nominal and older.
Operations, production weight, and constant-quality customer demand are different quantities.
d5.change_evidence: The latest NASS protected-crop census with physical production and sales by crop and system, combined with five-year grocery, foodservice, import, and facility-capacity forecasts, would materially improve the estimate.
d5.rationale: Demand for year-round local produce, supply resilience, land and water efficiency, controlled quality, institutional buying, and climate volatility supports growth. Cheap imports, high energy and capital costs, retailer bargaining, crop concentration, biological failures, and vertical-farm bankruptcies create downside.

caveats: The frozen dataset explicitly provides no defensible LMM firm count, so this conditional share cannot establish target volume.
The 2,994 operations threshold was only $10,000 in horticultural sales and operations are not firms.
e.change_evidence: NASS Census of Horticultural Specialties microdata matched to legal entity, crop mix, sales, operating expenses, ownership, facility leases, customer concentration, and normalized EBITDA would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: The 2019 horticulture census counted 2,994 operations selling protected food crops, but the frozen dataset provides no defensible LMM firm count. Conditional on the EBITDA band, independent greenhouse and hydroponic growers may have recurring external sales, while mixed floriculture businesses, small owner-operators, venture-backed vertical farms, captive retailer projects, distressed facilities, real-estate-heavy structures, and operations lacking transferable leases or contracts reduce eligibility.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($32,900) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Imports or consolidation can displace domestic operators without eliminating operator-required growing.
Some retail or foodservice buyers may vertically integrate supply, shifting rather than eliminating the operator.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of widely deployed autonomous greenhouse-as-a-service models in which technology vendors assume agronomic, food-safety, and customer-delivery accountability would lower the estimate.
o.rationale: Remaining food-crop demand requires an accountable grower to manage biological production, facilities, inputs, labor, food safety, traceability, harvest, packing, and delivery. AI and robotics can reduce staff but cannot become the licensed and commercially responsible farm operator within five years.

q.bridge: USDA ERS reports imports supplied about one-third of U.S. vegetable availability in 2025, with tomatoes and cucumbers heavily import-reliant and Mexico providing about 77% of fresh vegetable imports. I translate fresh-vegetable competition to covered-crop benefit retention. Retail and distributor buyers can compare delivered produce prices and renegotiate, compressing gains, while freshness, local supply, year-round reliability, food safety, specialty varieties, and direct channels can protect more value.
caveats: Vegetable-market evidence includes open-field production and processing vegetables.
Tomatoes, cucumbers, lettuce, herbs, berries, sprouts, and specialty crops have different pricing power and spoilage.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm customer contracts, weekly price and volume histories, import competition by crop and season, rejection and shrink data, and margin persistence after automation would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Wholesale produce pricing, perishability, buyer concentration, import competition, and crop oversupply pass efficiency gains through. Contracted local delivery, differentiated quality, branded packaging, scarce winter supply, and reduced spoilage preserve a minority.

caveats: Yield, quality, and energy improvements are not all labor substitution.
Glass greenhouses, low-tech high tunnels, hydroponics, sprouts, and vertical farms have different control and data maturity.
rho.change_evidence: Independent U.S. deployments reporting commissioning cost, climate and disease model accuracy, overrides, crop loss, energy and input use, yield, labor hours, and payback across multiple crop cycles would narrow the estimate.
rho.rationale: Controlled environments already collect temperature, humidity, light, irrigation, nutrient, energy, and crop data, favoring closed-loop decision support. Biological variability, disease risk, poor data interoperability, proprietary controls, energy volatility, crop-specific training data, food safety, fragile unit economics, and limited LMM technical capacity constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: The 2022 Census of Agriculture measures all U.S. farms, not eligible covered-crop firms, reporting an average producer age of 58.1 and 38% age 65 or older. The 2019 horticulture census reports 52% of operations were family- or individually owned but corporations produced 75% of sales. I translate aging and ownership structure into succession pressure, discounting because facilities may close, lease, recapitalize, or transfer within families rather than undergo a qualifying control sale. Corporate sales concentration may make all-farm age overstate LMM succession exposure.
caveats: All-farm producer age is not owner age or transaction intent.
Horticulture ownership data include floriculture and nursery operations beyond 111419.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and successor data for protected-crop operations plus PitchBook, Axial, greenhouse-industry, and property records distinguishing company sales from facility and asset transfers would replace the proxy.
s5.rationale: Founder succession, capital needs, energy costs, and strategic buyers seeking local supply support transfers. Specialized facilities, crop expertise, leases, customer concentration, food-safety records, and distressed vertical-farm economics can prevent transferable sales.

principal_driver: Continuous environmental and crop data make covered production well suited to AI-assisted climate, irrigation, disease, energy, and scheduling decisions.
principal_weakness: Energy and capital intensity, import competition, biological risk, and missing market-sizing inputs can overwhelm labor automation benefits.

caveats: S3 and S4 establish technical possibilities across specialty crops, not wage-weighted task exposure or realized five-year labor substitution in 111419; the packet appropriately labels a as PROXY, rho as ESTIMATE, and discloses the population mismatch.

S5 is a research-project description with prospective greenhouse demonstrations, so it supports sensor potential rather than observed adoption or implementation success; this limits rho but does not change the EVIDENCE_FIRST action.

S6's 2019 operation, sales, and ownership evidence is older and does not identify current eligible LMM firms or measure constant-quality five-year demand; the packet discloses these limitations and n remains MISSING.

S7 measures all-farm producers rather than protected-crop owners, successors, or transaction intent; the s5 proxy bridge states this mismatch, and correcting it cannot restore the missing F factor while n is MISSING.

S8 and S9 establish broad fresh-vegetable import competition but do not quantify firm-level retention of automation benefits across covered crops; q is therefore properly a broad PROXY with a wide range.

The very narrow 0.98-1.00 o range is judgmental and the cited sources do not directly quantify autonomous displacement of accountable farm operators, but o is labeled ESTIMATE and changing it within a plausible range would not alter the record's missing-base, EVIDENCE_FIRST status.

sources_audited.note: The Census NAICS page defines 111419 as food crops other than mushrooms grown under glass or protective cover and lists sprouts, fruit, hydroponic crops, melons, and vegetables as examples.

The ERS page reports the 2009-to-2019 increase from 1,476 to 2,994 CEA operations, the 56 percent production increase to 7.86 million hundredweight, the included controlled-production methods, and environmental factors controlled by these systems.

The NIFA page supports sensing, irrigation scheduling, deep-learning disease scanning in nursery or greenhouse devices, harvest assistance, and other automation, but its specialty-crop population is much broader than covered food crops and much of the evidence concerns orchards, vineyards, or research systems.

The project page supports development of sensing, mechanization, and automation to assist labor and management and reduce production costs, but it is broad specialty-crop research and does not measure realized deployment or labor substitution in 111419.

The project describes a greenhouse fiber-optic sensor network for micro-environment monitoring, productivity, quality, and energy efficiency, but describes proposed demonstrations rather than installed-fleet implementation outcomes.

The 2019 Census of Horticultural Specialties is the cited primary census source for protected-food-crop operations, sales, historical comparisons, and horticultural ownership structure; it supports the packet's census claims while remaining an older, broad horticultural source.

The 2022 profile reports 3,248,371 producers, 1,245,394 age 65 or older (about 38 percent), and an average age of 58.1, but it covers all U.S. farms and producers rather than owners of eligible protected-crop firms.

ERS reports that imports were about one-third of 2025 U.S. vegetable availability, that Mexico supplied about 77 percent of fresh vegetable imports excluding potatoes, and that tomatoes and cucumbers were highly import-reliant; the market scope includes open-field and processing vegetables.
ERS directly documents protected-culture imports from Mexico and Canada, near-year-round market-window creep, and increasing overlap with domestic fresh-vegetable production seasons.
